pages.title: FAQS
pages.body_html:
Find answers to common questions about Xinzuo kitchen knives, care, shipping, and warranties below. Can't find what you're looking for? Email us or message us on WhatsApp.

Quick Links

  • Knife care and maintenance guide
  • How to sharpen with a whetstone
  • Compare our knife series
  • Warranty information
  • Returns and exchanges
  • Chef knife buying guide

About Our Knives

What makes Xinzuo knives different from other brands?

Xinzuo knives are made in Yangjiang, China's knife-making capital with over 1,400 years of blade-smithing history. We use the same premium steels (VG10, 10Cr15CoMoV, 14Cr14MoVNb powder steel) found in high-end Japanese brands but without the heritage markup. Every knife ships from our Australian warehouse with a lifetime warranty. For a detailed comparison, see our guide to Chinese kitchen knives.

Where are Xinzuo knives made?

All Xinzuo knives are manufactured in Yangjiang, Guangdong Province, China. Yangjiang has been a centre for blade-making since the Tang Dynasty and produces kitchen knives for brands worldwide. Our factory uses modern CNC-controlled forging and vacuum heat treatment to ensure consistent quality across every blade.

Which series should I choose?

It depends on your priorities. The Supreme Series uses German 1.4116 stainless steel (reliable, low maintenance, and great value. The Retro Series steps up to Japanese-grade 10Cr15CoMoV steel with Damascus layering. The Mo Series adds 67-layer Damascus cladding over a VG10 core for striking aesthetics and performance. The Lan Series uses 14Cr14MoVNb powder steel at 62 HRC) our hardest, sharpest option. Not sure where to start? Browse our best sellers to see what other customers choose. Our series comparison page has a detailed breakdown.

What is Damascus steel?

Damascus steel refers to blades made by forge-welding multiple layers of different steel alloys together. The folding and hammering process creates the distinctive wavy pattern visible on the blade surface. Our Damascus knives use 67 or 73 layers of steel surrounding a high-carbon core. The pattern is genuine (not cosmetically etched) and each knife is unique. Learn more in our real vs fake Damascus guide.

What does HRC mean?

HRC stands for Rockwell Hardness C scale, the standard measure of steel hardness in knife-making. Higher HRC means the steel holds a sharper edge longer but is more brittle. German knives typically rate 56-58 HRC, Japanese knives 60-62 HRC, and powder steels can reach 64+ HRC. Our steel hardness guide explains what different HRC ratings mean for daily use.

Knife Care

Can I put my knife in the dishwasher?

No (and this applies to all quality kitchen knives, not just ours. Dishwashers use harsh detergents and high-pressure water that damage blade edges and can corrode steel over time. The heat also warps handles. Hand wash with warm soapy water, dry immediately with a towel, and store properly. It takes 10 seconds and protects your investment for life.

How often should I sharpen my knife?

For most home cooks, sharpening on a whetstone every 3-6 months is sufficient. Between sharpenings, use a honing steel or strop before each cooking session to realign the edge. Harder steels (like VG10 at 60 HRC or powder steel at 62 HRC) hold their edge longer and need less frequent sharpening. Our whetstone sharpening guide covers the full process.

What whetstone grit should I use?

Start with a 1000-grit stone for regular sharpening) it removes enough steel to restore a dull edge without being overly aggressive. Follow with a 3000 or 6000-grit stone to polish and refine the edge. A 1000/6000 combination stone covers most home kitchen needs. Browse our sharpening whetstones or see our whetstone grit guide for detailed recommendations.

How should I store my knives?

The two best options are magnetic knife holders (wall-mounted strips or freestanding blocks) and in-drawer knife trays. Both protect the blade edge by preventing contact with other utensils. Avoid loose storage in drawers where blades can knock together, dulling edges and creating safety hazards. Our knife storage comparison breaks down the pros and cons of each method.

Do Damascus knives need special care?

Damascus knives need the same basic care as any quality kitchen knife: hand wash, dry immediately, store properly. The Damascus pattern is created during forging and won't wash off or fade with normal use. Avoid leaving acidic foods on the blade for extended periods, as this can cause discolouration. For pattern care tips, see our Damascus steel care guide.

Choosing the Right Knife

What is the best first knife to buy?

An 8-inch chef knife or 7-inch santoku. Both handle the majority of kitchen tasks (chopping, slicing, dicing, and mincing. The chef knife's curved blade suits rocking cuts; the santoku's flatter profile is better for up-and-down chopping. Choose whichever feels more natural in your hand. Browse our best starter knives or read our beginner's buying guide for the full decision process.

What is the difference between a santoku and a chef knife?

The main differences are blade profile and cutting technique. Chef knives have a curved blade designed for rocking cuts and are typically 8-10 inches long. Santoku knives have a flatter blade suited to push-cutting and are usually 7 inches. Santokus are lighter and more manoeuvrable; chef knives handle larger ingredients more efficiently. Our santoku vs chef knife comparison has the full breakdown.

Should I buy a knife set or individual knives?

It depends on your budget and how specific your needs are. Sets offer better value per knife and give you a curated collection. Individual knives let you choose exactly the blade you want and build your collection over time. Most home cooks need three knives: a chef/santoku, a utility knife, and a paring knife. Browse our knife sets or see our sets vs individual knives guide.

What knife do I need for vegetables?

A nakiri (Japanese vegetable knife) is purpose-built for vegetable prep) the flat, rectangular blade makes clean, precise cuts. A santoku is the versatile alternative that handles vegetables and proteins equally well. For heavy-duty vegetable work like pumpkin and sweet potato, a Chinese cleaver provides the weight and blade surface to power through dense produce. Our vegetable knife guide compares all the options.

Shipping & Orders

How fast do you ship?

All orders placed before 2 PM AEST on a business day are dispatched the same day from our Australian warehouse. Regular Post ($9.95) arrives in 2-5 business days. Express Post (Calculated at checkout) arrives in 1-2 business days. You'll receive a tracking number via email as soon as your order ships. See our shipping page for full details.

Do you offer free shipping?

Yes (free shipping is available on orders over a certain amount. Check the current threshold on our shipping page or at checkout.

What is your return policy?

We offer a 30-day return policy. If you're not satisfied with your purchase, contact us within 30 days for a full refund. Items must be unused and in original packaging. See our returns and exchanges page for the full process.

What does the lifetime warranty cover?

Our lifetime warranty covers manufacturing defects in materials and workmanship. This includes blade delamination, handle cracking under normal use, and premature corrosion. It does not cover damage from misuse (dropping, prying, dishwasher damage, cutting on glass or stone surfaces). Full details are on our warranty page.

Product Details

What is the sharpening angle on your knives?

Our Japanese-style knives (Mo, Retro, Lan, Zhen series) are sharpened to 15 degrees per side. The Supreme Series uses a slightly wider 18-degree angle for added durability with softer German steel. When sharpening at home, maintain the original angle for best performance. Our sharpening angle guide explains the differences.

Are your knives full tang?

It depends on the series. Our riveted handle knives (like the Supreme, Mo, Retro, and Elegant series) use full tang construction, where the steel runs the full length of the handle and is secured with rivets. Our Japanese-style knives with octagonal handles (like the Lan Series) use hidden tang construction, where the blade steel extends into the handle but is concealed by the wood. Hidden tang is a deliberate design choice common in traditional Japanese knives. It shifts weight forward toward the blade, which gives a lighter, more agile feel in the hand and makes the knife easier to control during long prep sessions. Both construction methods are durable and built to last.

pages.title: Maintenance Guide
pages.body_html:
Proper care keeps your Xinzuo knives performing at their best for years. Follow these simple steps to maintain sharpness, appearance, and safety.

1. Cleaning
- Always hand wash your knives immediately after use.
- Use mild dish soap and warm water with a soft sponge.
- Dry thoroughly with a soft towel before storing.
- Do not put knives in the dishwasher - heat, moisture, and detergents can damage the blade and handle.

2. Drying
Moisture is one of the main causes of rust or corrosion. Always ensure your knife is completely dry before storage, especially around the handle and bolster area.

3. Storage
- Store knives safely in a knife block or magnetic strip, or a protective sheath.
- Avoid keeping knives loose in drawers to prevent dulling or accidental injury.
- Ensure storage areas are dry and well-ventilated.
- Read our guide on magnetic holders vs knife blocks to find the best option for your kitchen.

4. Sharpening
- Regular honing keeps your knife edge aligned. Use a honing rod every few uses.
- For sharpening, use a whetstone or have your knives serviced by a professional. Our whetstone sharpening guide walks you through the full process.
- Check our sharpening angle guide to find the correct angle for your knife.
- Avoid pull-through sharpeners unless specifically designed for high-quality steel knives.

5. Cutting Surfaces
- Use wooden or soft plastic cutting boards to preserve blade edges. Our guide on end grain vs edge grain boards explains which type is gentlest on your knives.
- Avoid cutting on hard surfaces such as glass, metal, or stone.

6. Proper Use
- Use each knife for its intended purpose - avoid twisting, prying, or cutting frozen foods or bones with fine-edged knives. Not sure which knife to use? Check our Japanese knife types guide.
- Keep blades away from extreme temperatures and chemicals.

7. Long-Term Care
- Lightly oil carbon steel blades with food-safe mineral oil to prevent oxidation. See our Damascus steel care guide for detailed instructions.
- Regular maintenance keeps both stainless and carbon steel blades performing like new.

Need Help?
If you have questions about knife care or sharpening, contact us at:
Email: support@xinzuo.com.au
Phone: +61 493 005 283
Or browse our frequently asked questions.

pages.title: Warranty
pages.body_html:
Xinzuo Limited Lifetime Warranty

Every Xinzuo knife is built to last. We stand behind the quality of our materials and craftsmanship with a Limited Lifetime Warranty for the original purchaser.

What Is Covered

This warranty covers defects in materials and workmanship under normal household use, including:

  • Blade defects such as cracks, voids, or delamination in the steel
  • Incorrect hardness or uneven tempering of the blade
  • Handle defects including loose rivets, splitting, or separation from the tang
  • Incorrect blade geometry from the manufacturing process

What Is Not Covered

This warranty does not cover damage resulting from misuse, neglect, accidents, or normal wear and tear, including:

  • Chipping or breaking caused by cutting bones, frozen foods, hard seeds, or other hard materials
  • Using the knife as a pry bar, screwdriver, or for any purpose other than food preparation
  • Rust or corrosion resulting from improper care (e.g. leaving wet, storing damp, dishwasher use)
  • Damage caused by dishwashers, including handle discolouration and blade corrosion
  • Normal dulling of the edge through regular use
  • Cosmetic wear such as scratches, patina development, or surface marks
  • Damage from dropping, impact, or accidental mishandling
  • Damage from improper sharpening or modification
  • Damage from professional or commercial kitchen use (warranty covers household use only)

How to Make a Claim

To submit a warranty claim, email us at support@xinzuo.com.au with:

  • Your order number or proof of purchase
  • Clear photos of the defect
  • A brief description of the issue and how it occurred

Our team will assess your claim and respond within 3 business days. If we need to inspect the knife, we will provide a return shipping address. Please do not send items back without contacting us first.

Warranty Remedy

If a manufacturing defect is confirmed, Xinzuo will, at our discretion, either repair or replace the product free of charge. Replacement products may be a current equivalent model if the original is no longer available.

Important Notes

  • This warranty applies to the original purchaser only and is not transferable.
  • Proof of purchase from xinzuo.com.au or an authorised Xinzuo retailer is required.
  • This warranty is in addition to your rights under Australian Consumer Law. Nothing in this warranty limits or excludes any consumer guarantees or other rights you may have under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth).

articles.title: Whetstone Guide: Pick the Right Stone for Your Kitchen Knives
articles.body_html:
Whetstone Guide: Pick the Right Stone for Your Kitchen Knives

What Whetstone Should You Actually Buy?

For 90% of Australian home cooks, the right whetstone is a 1000/6000 grit aluminium-oxide combination stone with a non-slip base, splash-and-go (no soaking), priced between $35 and $80. Buy that, learn it, and ignore everything else for the first two years. The stone we sell that does this job is the 1000/6000 Whetstone with Bamboo Base at $44.95. That's the recommendation. The rest of this guide explains why.

Quick answer: One stone. Two grits on the same brick. 1000 to sharpen, 6000 to finish. Splash-and-go. Bamboo base or rubber feet. Done. Skip the diamond plates, oil stones, and 12-stone progressions until you have a knife that actually needs them.

Most whetstone guides try to teach you everything about every kind of sharpening stone. The result is a 4,000-word article that leaves a beginner more confused than before they started reading. This one is the opposite. I'm going to tell you what to buy, why, and what you can safely ignore. I sharpen the entire factory test set every few months on the same stone I'm about to recommend, and I've watched hundreds of customers get sharper knives out of one good combination stone than out of a $400 setup they couldn't make sense of.

Why Is a 1000/6000 Combination Stone the Right First Whetstone?

Because it covers everything a normal kitchen knife needs and nothing it doesn't. The 1000 grit side resharpens a dulled edge in five to ten minutes. The 6000 grit side polishes that edge to a clean, push-cut finish that suits the harder Japanese-style steels (59 to 64 HRC) we sell. One stone, two jobs, one purchase.

The reason this combination works for almost everyone is that the gap between 1000 and 6000 is the working range for kitchen sharpening. Anything coarser than 1000 is repair-grit, used for chips and damaged edges, and most home cooks never need it. Anything finer than 6000 is enthusiast territory, used for natural-stone polishing on hand-forged single-bevel blades, and home cooks definitely don't need it.

If you sharpen a softer 54 to 57 HRC blade like a German Wusthof or a Victorinox, the 6000 polish is overkill but not harmful. Stop at 4000-equivalent feel and call it done. If you sharpen a harder 60 to 64 HRC blade, like our Lan Series powder-steel knives, the 6000 finish is exactly where you want to land. Same stone, both jobs.

This is the same logic Larrin Thomas at Knife Steel Nerds arrives at when he tests CATRA edge retention against grit progression. Beyond a 1000-grit base sharpen, the marginal cutting performance gains shrink fast. The polish helps, the visual finish improves, but the edge already cuts at 1000.

What Are the Different Types of Sharpening Stone?

There are four real categories: water stones, oil stones, ceramic stones, and diamond plates. For a kitchen knife, you want a synthetic water stone (which most modern combination stones are). The other three exist for specific reasons that don't apply to home cooking.

Stone type What it is Best for Skip if
Synthetic water stone Aluminium-oxide grit in a softer binder, used with water Kitchen knives, fast feedback, easy to learn on You're sharpening axes or lawn-mower blades
Oil stone Arkansas novaculite or India aluminium oxide, used with honing oil Pocket knives, woodworking chisels, slow polishing You want fast sharpening on hard kitchen steel
Ceramic stone Sintered alumina or zirconium, used dry or with water Quick touch-ups, edge maintenance, high-HRC steels You need to remove real metal from a dull blade
Diamond plate Industrial diamond bonded to a steel plate, stays dead flat Flattening other stones, repairing chipped edges, very hard powder steels You don't already own three water stones

Why Synthetic Water Stones Win for Kitchen Knives

They cut fast, they release fresh abrasive as you work (which keeps the surface biting), and they give you immediate feedback through the slurry. You can feel the burr forming. You can see the steel coming off into the milky liquid on top of the stone. That feedback loop is how beginners learn what a sharp edge feels like under their fingers.

The trade-off is that water stones dish out. The middle of the stone wears faster than the edges because that's where the blade lives. After 50 to 100 sharpenings, you'll see a visible hollow in the surface. You flatten it back with a flattening stone or a cheap diamond plate. That's the only real maintenance the stone itself needs.

Ceramic stones don't dish as fast, which is genuinely useful, but they're slower to remove metal and they cost more. Buy a ceramic stone as your second or third stone, not your first.

Where Diamond and Oil Stones Actually Belong

Diamond plates have one job in a home kitchen: flattening your water stones. A 400-grit Atoma or DMT plate runs $80 to $200, sits in a drawer, and earns its keep once a year when you flatten the dish out of your 1000/6000. Don't buy a diamond plate to sharpen kitchen knives directly. The aggressive grit chews up the soft cladding on Damascus blades and leaves a rougher edge than a 1000-grit water stone.

Oil stones suit traditional craftsmen, especially woodworkers and people maintaining pocket knives. They cut slower than water stones, they're messier (oil instead of water), and they don't release fresh grit, so they polish more than they sharpen at higher grits. For kitchen knives, water stones win on every metric.

Which Whetstone Grit Do You Need for Kitchen Knives?

1000 grit for sharpening, 5000 to 6000 grit for finishing, and that's it for the first two years of your sharpening life. Don't buy a 220, don't buy an 8000, don't buy a 12000 natural stone. Master the 1000/6000 first.

Grit Job When you need it
220 to 400 Repair Chipped tip, rolled edge, or you're regrinding a damaged bevel. Almost never.
800 to 1200 Sharpen Standard resharpening every 2 to 4 months. This is your workhorse.
3000 to 5000 Refine After 1000-grit work, smooths the burr and tightens the edge.
6000 to 8000 Polish Mirror finish for high-HRC Japanese-style steel where the polish actually shows on the edge.

The argument for stopping at 6000 instead of climbing to 8000 is that the marginal sharpness gain doesn't justify the extra stone or the extra time. Oishya, the Polish-Japanese knife retailer, makes a useful point: if you're cutting meat, stop between 4000 and 6000, because a polished edge can roll on muscle fibre. A slightly toothier 4000 to 6000 edge bites into protein better than a mirror-polished 8000. For vegetable work, either grit cuts cleanly. For 90% of home cooking, 6000 is the right ceiling.

Our whetstone grit guide for kitchen knives goes deeper into the grit-by-grit rationale if you want the longer version.

Do You Need to Soak a Whetstone Before Use?

Most modern combination stones, including ours, are splash-and-go. Wet the surface for 30 seconds, sharpen, top up with water, wipe dry. You don't soak them. The exception is traditional soaker stones like the original King 1000/6000 and some older Naniwa Super Stones, which need a 10-minute soak until air bubbles stop. Read the manufacturer's instructions before you do anything else.

Why splash-and-go won the modern market: soaking a synthetic stone for hours, or storing one wet, breaks down the binder. The grit particles release too easily, the stone wears faster, and in a humid Australian climate it can develop mould patches in the body. A splash-and-go stone gives you the same cutting feel without the soak-time hassle.

The one universal rule: never soak a 3000+ finishing stone unless the manufacturer specifically tells you to. Splash water on the surface only. Finishing stones use harder binders and longer soaks can crack them. Sakai Ichimonji Mitsuhide, one of the long-running Japanese sharpening houses, makes this point in their whetstone care guidance, and it applies to every brand.

How to Tell if Your Stone Is a Soaker or Splash-and-Go

  • Modern combination stones priced under $80: assume splash-and-go unless the box says otherwise.
  • Older King-brand and traditional Naniwa Super Stones: soaker, 10 to 15 minutes.
  • Shapton Pro and Shapton Glass: splash-and-go.
  • All four of our stones: splash-and-go. Wet the surface, sharpen, dry.

Why Does the Base or Frame Actually Matter?

Because a stone that slides around while you sharpen will give you an inconsistent bevel, and an inconsistent bevel is a dull knife. The base or frame keeps the stone locked in place so the angle stays stable for the 5 to 10 minutes you spend on each side of each blade.

Three options work. A bamboo or wooden base with rubber feet (what comes on our 1000/6000 stone), a damp tea towel under the stone on a flat bench, or a dedicated stone holder with a non-slip base. All three solve the same problem. Don't sharpen on a bare benchtop, especially not on stone or polished timber. The stone will skate, your angle will wander, and your edge will end up worse than when you started.

Worth knowing: The bamboo base on our 1000/6000 stone has a second job. After sharpening, lift the stone off the base and rest both pieces separately on a tea towel for 24 hours. Air gets underneath the stone and dries it from both sides. A stone stored damp in a cupboard will mould in our climate within a few months.

Which Xinzuo Whetstone Should You Buy?

Four stones. Two singles for first-time buyers, two complete sets for people who want the full kit on day one. All four ship from our Sydney warehouse with free shipping over $100, and all four come under the same lifetime warranty as the knives they're built to sharpen.

The Two Single Stones

The 1000/5000 Premium Whetstone at $34.95 is the cheapest entry point. Aluminium-oxide synthetic, 80mm wide, splash-and-go. If you want to try whetstone sharpening without committing to a full kit, this is the stone. The 5000 finish is plenty for German-steel knives at 54 to 57 HRC where a finer polish would be wasted.

The 1000/6000 Whetstone with Bamboo Base at $44.95 is the one I recommend most often. Same 1000-grit sharpening side, but a finer 6000-grit polish that suits the harder 59 to 64 HRC steels we sell. The bamboo base doubles as a non-slip mount and a drying rack. For ten dollars more than the cheaper stone, you get a better finish, a non-slip mount, and the right setup for harder Japanese-style steel. This is the stone for most people.

The Two Complete Sets

The 1000/5000 Premium Whetstone Set at $74.95 includes the 1000/5000 combination stone, a flattening stone (you'll wear a dish into the surface eventually and need to flatten it back), a leather strop for finishing, and an angle guide for keeping a consistent bevel while you learn. If you're starting from zero and you want every piece in one box, buy this. The angle guide alone saves a beginner three weeks of trial and error.

The 3000/8000 Premium Whetstone Set at $74.95 is the polishing companion to the 1000/5000 set. Buy this second, after you've sharpened on the 1000/5000 for a year and you've moved up to a harder powder-steel blade where the 8000 finish actually pays off. Most home cooks don't need it. People who own a Lan Series knife and like to sharpen do.

Which Knives Pair With Each Stone?

Match the grit to the steel hardness. Softer steel takes a lower-polish edge well; harder steel rewards a finer polish. Anything in our whetstone-friendly range works on the stones above, but the right pairing makes the difference between an edge that's good enough and an edge that's actually optimal.

Knife steel HRC Right stone for the job
German 1.4116, X50CrMoV15 54 to 57 1000/5000 single stone or set
10Cr15CoMoV (VG-10 equivalent) 60 to 62 1000/6000 with bamboo base
14Cr14MoVNb powder steel 62 to 64 1000/6000 then graduate to 3000/8000 set

For technique once the stone is on the bench, our step-by-step whetstone sharpening guide walks through the full motion. The knife sharpening angle guide covers what bevel to use for each steel, and the honing steel guide covers the in-between maintenance that keeps a sharp edge sharp longer.

What Mistakes Should You Avoid With a Whetstone?

  1. Buying a $20 unbranded stone. The grit ratings are made up, the binder is too soft, the stone dishes within ten sharpenings, and you'll learn bad technique because the feedback is inconsistent. Spend the extra $15 to $25 on a stone with a real grit number and a manufacturer who'll stand behind it.
  2. Storing the stone wet in a cupboard. Especially in Brisbane, Sydney, or any humid coastal city. The stone will absorb moisture, develop mould patches in the binder, and crack when it dries unevenly in the next summer heat. Air-dry on a tea towel for 24 hours, then store somewhere ventilated.
  3. Skipping the flattening step. A dished stone teaches you a curved bevel, which produces a duller knife than a flat stone with poor technique. Flatten every five to ten sharpening sessions, or whenever you can see a visible hollow.
  4. Buying a 220-grit coarse stone before you need it. Coarse stones are repair-grit. If your knife isn't chipped or damaged, a 220 will eat metal faster than you mean to and leave you with a thinner blade than you started with. Skip until you actually have something to repair.
  5. Using a pull-through carbide sharpener alongside the stone. The pull-through grinds an inconsistent bevel that the stone then has to fix. Pick one. If you've got a whetstone, throw the pull-through in the drawer and forget it exists.

How Often Should You Sharpen on a Whetstone?

Every two to four months for a regularly used kitchen knife, more often if you cook nightly, less often if your knife sits in a block most of the week. The honest answer is: sharpen when the knife stops cutting cleanly through a tomato under its own weight. That's the test. If you have to push the blade down through a ripe tomato, the edge is gone.

Between sharpenings, keep the edge true with a ceramic honing rod. Five passes per side, every few uses, takes 15 seconds. The rod realigns the edge without removing metal. The stone restores the edge by removing metal. Two different jobs, two different tools.

For a working knife in a domestic kitchen, that rhythm of weekly honing plus quarterly stone work will keep a 60-HRC blade sharper than 95% of home knives in Australia. The lifetime warranty on our knives covers the steel; the technique is on you, and a good stone makes the technique possible.

Shop All Whetstones

Sources and Further Reading

  • Larrin Thomas. Knife Steel Nerds. CATRA edge-retention testing on grit progression and the diminishing returns of higher-polish edges.
  • Sakai Ichimonji Mitsuhide. Whetstone care and storage. Drying and soaking guidance from a long-running Japanese sharpening house.

Related Articles

  • Whetstone Grit Guide for Kitchen Knives
  • How to Sharpen Knives on a Whetstone
  • Knife Sharpening Angle Guide
  • Honing Steel Guide: Knife Edge Maintenance

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best whetstone for kitchen knives in Australia?

A 1000/6000 grit aluminium-oxide combination stone with a non-slip base is the best first whetstone for almost every Australian home cook. The 1000 side resharpens, the 6000 polishes, and one stone covers everything you'll do for the first two years. Our 1000/6000 with bamboo base sells at $44.95 with free shipping over $100.

Do I need a single whetstone or a complete set?

A single combination stone is enough for most home cooks. The complete sets add a flattening stone, leather strop and angle guide, which save beginners time but add about $30 to $40 to the price. If you're confident learning by feel, buy the single stone. If you want every piece on day one, buy the 1000/5000 Premium Whetstone Set.

What grit whetstone do I need for a Japanese-style knife?

1000 grit to sharpen and 5000 to 6000 grit to finish covers Japanese-style blades from 59 to 62 HRC. For powder steels at 62 to 64 HRC, the 6000 finish is the right ceiling and an 8000 polish is worth the extra step. Below 59 HRC, stop at 5000 because the steel won't hold a finer polish without rolling.

Do you have to soak a whetstone before sharpening?

Most modern combination stones, including all four of ours, are splash-and-go. Wet the surface for 30 seconds, sharpen, top up with water, wipe dry. Older soaker stones like the original King 1000/6000 need 10 to 15 minutes in water until air bubbles stop. Read the manufacturer's instructions before the first use.

Can you sharpen a serrated knife on a whetstone?

No. A flat whetstone only contacts the back of a serrated edge and will round the points off the scallops. Use a tapered ceramic rod or a dedicated serrated sharpener for bread knives and steak knives, or send them to a professional sharpener once a year. Most steak and bread knives only need sharpening every two to three years if used carefully.

How do you know when a whetstone needs flattening?

Lay a steel ruler flat across the surface and look for light gaps under the middle. A visible dish means the stone is no longer flat and is teaching you a curved bevel. Flatten every five to ten sharpenings using a flattening stone or a 400-grit diamond plate. Our 1000/5000 Premium Whetstone Set includes a flattening stone for this.

articles.title: VG10 Steel Explained: The Workhorse of Premium Damascus Knives
articles.body_html:
VG10 is a Japanese cutlery steel from Takefu Special Steel, with about 1% carbon, 15% chromium, 1% molybdenum, 1.5% cobalt and 0.2% vanadium, hardened to 60 to 62 HRC. It became the default cutting core for premium Damascus knives because it sits right in the middle of every trade-off that matters: hard enough to hold a 12 to 15 degree edge for months, tough enough to survive home use without chipping, and well-mannered enough on a whetstone that you can sharpen it at your kitchen sink without specialist gear.

Quick answer: VG10 is a Japanese-grade stainless cutting steel from Takefu, run at 60 to 62 HRC, with cobalt and molybdenum added to a 1% carbon, 15% chromium base. It is the most common cutting core in premium Damascus knives because it balances edge retention, corrosion resistance and ease of resharpening better than any other steel in its price tier.

Important framing before we go further: Xinzuo and Hezhen knives use VG10 and its Chinese-made twin 10Cr15CoMoV as cutting cores, but the knives themselves are forged in Yangjiang, China. The steel is Japanese-grade. The knife is Yangjiang-made. I will not pretend otherwise, and you should be wary of any retailer who does.

What is VG10 steel?

VG10 is a high-alloy martensitic stainless cutting steel developed by Takefu Special Steel in Fukui Prefecture around 1959. The name reads "V Gold 10," Takefu's premium-grade designation. Larrin Thomas at Knife Steel Nerds traces it back to roughly the same era as 154CM in the United States, which makes VG10 one of the older Japanese stainless steels still in continuous production.

The recipe was unusual when it appeared. Takefu added cobalt to a high-carbon, high-chromium stainless base to improve tempering resistance and let the steel keep its hardness with high-temperature coatings. The same cobalt addition turned out to do something useful for kitchen knives: it strengthens the matrix and gives the heat treater more headroom to push hardness without the grain getting coarse. Takefu sells VG10 as cold-rolled strip stock to forging houses across Japan and, increasingly, across Asia, most often as a clad core sandwiched between softer cladding layers and forge-welded into the Damascus pattern. The cladding is decorative and protective. The VG10 is what cuts.

Why did VG10 become the default core for premium Damascus?

It became the default because it solved three problems at once: edge retention long enough to feel premium, corrosion resistance good enough for kitchen abuse, and machinability good enough that mid-sized forgers could grind it without ruining the heat treatment.

That third one matters more than buyers realise. A steel can be exceptional on paper and still fail commercially if forgers cannot work it consistently. VG10 has fine, evenly distributed chromium carbides that polish cleanly, hold a fine edge without crumbling, and respond predictably to oil quenching followed by a low temper. CPM-S35VN takes a finer edge but eats grinding belts. AUS-10 is similar in chemistry but has slightly lower toughness and a less refined grain. ZDP-189 holds a cleaner edge for longer but chips more easily and costs three times as much. VG10 has no single category where it wins outright. It just loses none of them badly enough to drop out of the mix.

The honest framing: VG10 is a B-plus across every category that matters for a kitchen knife, and an A in the categories most home cooks actually care about. It is not the absolute best at anything, and that is precisely why it has stayed dominant.

What does each element in VG10 actually do?

Every line on the spec sheet is doing useful work. The steel is fine-tuned, not over-engineered.

Element Typical % What it contributes
Carbon (C) 0.95 to 1.05 Hardness and edge retention. Bonds with chromium and vanadium to form wear-resistant carbides.
Chromium (Cr) 14.5 to 15.0 Stainlessness. Above ~12% free chromium the steel forms a passive oxide layer that resists kitchen acids.
Cobalt (Co) 1.30 to 1.50 Tempering resistance. Lets the heat treater push higher hardness without grain growth or brittleness.
Molybdenum (Mo) 0.90 to 1.20 Pitting corrosion resistance and edge stability. Forms its own hard carbides.
Vanadium (V) 0.10 to 0.30 Grain refinement. Vanadium carbides pin grain boundaries and produce a finer, tougher matrix.

Carbon carries the workload on hardness. Chromium does the rust resistance. Molybdenum and vanadium are the fine-tuners that produce the clean, polished edge VG10 is known for. And cobalt, the unusual one, is what allowed Takefu to push the working hardness to 60 to 62 HRC without the brittleness you would normally expect at that level.

Why cobalt is the quiet hero of the recipe

Cobalt is the element that earns VG10 its reputation for staying sharp under sustained work. At only 1.5% it sounds trivial, but cobalt is a hot-hardness booster, not a bulk hardener. It strengthens the ferrite matrix and resists tempering when the steel gets warm under load. That stability is the difference between a knife that holds an edge for two weeks and one that holds it for two months. The carbides do the abrasion resistance. The cobalt-strengthened matrix keeps those carbides in their proper place while you work.

What does 60 to 62 HRC mean at the cutting board?

HRC is the Rockwell C hardness scale, and 60 to 62 puts VG10 in the productive middle of the kitchen steel range. Hard enough to hold an acute 12 to 15 degree edge for two to three months of daily use. Soft enough to flex slightly when it hits a stray fruit pit instead of chipping. Easy enough to resharpen on a 1000-grit whetstone in five minutes.

For context, German 1.4116 sits at 54 to 58 HRC. Powder steels like 14Cr14MoVNb (the core of our Lan Series) run at 62 to 64. VG10 in the middle is no accident. It is where edge retention, stability and resharpening ease all converge for a normal home cook.

I notice the difference most clearly in two places. The first is when the knife arrives: a factory-fresh VG10 edge slides through onion skin under its own weight in a way a 1.4116 blade simply cannot. The second is six months later. Where the German blade has gone from "glides" to "requires real downward pressure," the VG10 has gone from "glides" to "glides with a tiny bit of help." Same starting point, very different decay curve. The knife steel hardness guide covers what HRC numbers mean and why they are only half the story.

What is VG10 actually good for, and what is it not?

VG10 is a precision slicing steel. It excels at the work most home cooks spend most of their kitchen time doing, and it falls down on the work you should not be doing with any thin, hard blade in the first place.

It earns its keep on precision vegetable work, long prep sessions, boneless protein and acidic ingredients. Onion paper, tomato skin, carrot brunoise, fish fillets, citrus, vinegar reductions. The fine edge plus clean carbide matrix produces slices that release off the blade rather than dragging, and the cobalt-strengthened matrix keeps the edge stable through 30 to 45 minutes of continuous work. The 15% chromium plus 1% molybdenum means you genuinely do not see staining or pitting in real kitchen use unless you abuse it.

It falls down on heavy chopping into bone, frozen food, twisting cuts, glass or ceramic cutting boards, and the dishwasher. The thin geometry plus high hardness means lateral force chips the edge instead of rolling it. Use a cleaver for the rough work. Our cleaver versus chef knife guide covers when to switch tools. None of those limitations are unique to VG10. They apply to every premium Japanese-style cutting steel above 58 HRC.

How does VG10 age over months of real use?

VG10 ages well. The cutting performance degrades on a long, gentle slope rather than a sudden cliff, and the cosmetic surfaces pick up patina rather than rusting through.

From my own logbook running a Mo Series 8.5" chef knife and a Yu Series 8" chef knife on parallel daily use for a year:

  • Weeks 1 to 4: Factory edge. The blade slides through onion paper without flexing it. No pressure needed for tomatoes, citrus or boneless protein.
  • Months 2 to 3: Working edge. Tomato slicing now wants a tiny amount of forward motion, not just downward pressure. A 30-second pass on a ceramic rod resets the bevel.
  • Months 4 to 6: First sharpening. Five minutes on a 1000-grit whetstone followed by a 3000-grit polish. Back to factory-fresh.
  • Months 7 to 12: Second sharpening cycle. The cladding has picked up faint surface marks but no rust. The cutting bevel polishes back as cleanly as it did the first time.

That is realistic edge life for a serious home cook who hand washes, dries immediately and uses the right honing rod. Heavier users in commercial kitchens sharpen more often, but the steel responds the same way every time. There is no breakdown point where it suddenly becomes harder to bring back. On the rust question, Larrin Thomas's saltwater testing rated VG10 at roughly 7.9 out of 10 for corrosion resistance, with about 11.7% free chromium and 0.9% molybdenum remaining in solution after carbide formation. None of my VG10 or 10Cr15CoMoV blades have ever rusted in normal hand-wash-and-dry use.

How does VG10 compare to the steels next to it?

The useful comparison is not VG10 against random benchmark steels, but against the three alternatives a buyer is most likely to be choosing between in the $80 to $200 range.

Property VG10 10Cr15CoMoV 14Cr14MoVNb German 1.4116
Origin Japan (Takefu) China (GB standard) China (powder steel) Germany / Solingen
Working hardness 60 to 62 HRC 60 to 62 HRC 62 to 64 HRC 54 to 58 HRC
Edge retention Very good Very good Excellent Fair
Toughness Moderate Moderate Moderate to low High
Corrosion resistance Very good Very good Good Excellent
Sharpening ease Easy on whetstone Easy on whetstone Slower, needs finer grit Easy with anything
Price tier (clad strip) $$ $ $$$ $

VG10 and 10Cr15CoMoV deserve their own section because the question of how they relate is the single most asked thing about modern Damascus knives.

Is VG10 the same as 10Cr15CoMoV?

The two steels share a near-identical chemical recipe. Carbon, chromium, cobalt, molybdenum and vanadium all match within fractions of a percent. The Chinese GB-standard 10Cr15CoMoV was developed as a domestic equivalent to VG10, and reference sites like zKnives list them as direct equivalents. Same alloy family, same grain structure, same job description.

The differences are in heat treatment and manufacturing track record, not the recipe. Takefu has sixty years of refined quench and temper protocols for VG10 strip. Chinese mills producing 10Cr15CoMoV are now producing genuinely comparable strip stock, and reputable forges in Yangjiang follow the same austenitise-quench-double-temper cycle Takefu specifies. In side-by-side use I cannot tell them apart by feel. I have written the longer head-to-head in VG10 vs 10Cr15CoMoV if you want the full chemistry comparison. The alloy is the recipe, the heat treatment is the cooking, and both matter equally.

Which Xinzuo and Hezhen knives use VG10-tier cutting cores?

Most of the premium Damascus range at Xinzuo Australia uses either VG10 or its alloy twin 10Cr15CoMoV at 60 to 62 HRC, sandwiched in 67-layer Damascus cladding. The Hezhen B38L Elegant Series uses Takefu VG10 directly. The Mo (X06), Yi (B27), Yu (B13R) and Pin (X020) series use 10Cr15CoMoV at the same hardness and the same cladding spec, made in Yangjiang. The cutting performance is functionally identical across the range, and you can see the full lineup in the VG10 steel knives collection.

If you want a starting recommendation: the Mo Series 8.5" chef knife at $129.95 is the most popular entry point. The Mo Series 7.5" santoku at $94.95 uses the same core. For the rosewood-handle look, the Yu Series 8" chef knife at $119.95 is a fair price for a hand-forged Damascus chef knife in this performance tier. All three live in the broader Damascus knives range.

Worth saying explicitly: when you see a Xinzuo or Hezhen knife described as a "VG10 knife" or "VG10 core," verify whether the actual core steel is Japanese Takefu VG10 or Chinese 10Cr15CoMoV. The performance is comparable, the price differs, and an honest retailer will tell you which one you are buying. The collection page on this site spells out the distinction. Some others do not.

Shop VG10 Steel Knives

How do you sharpen and maintain a VG10 blade?

A 1000-grit whetstone followed by a 3000-grit finishing stone is everything you need. Hold the blade at 12 to 15 degrees per side, raise a small burr on each side, polish it off on the finer stone, and the steel will reward you with an edge that pops arm hair. VG10 polishes cleanly without grabbing the stone or smearing into the cladding.

Three rules for every VG10 and 10Cr15CoMoV knife in my kitchen:

  1. Ceramic honing rod, not a steel rod. A traditional steel rod can microchip the edge of any cutting steel above 58 HRC. Ceramic realigns the edge gently and adds weeks between full sharpenings.
  2. Hand wash and dry immediately. The dishwasher's heat plus alkaline detergent plus items knocking together will wreck both the edge and the cladding pattern.
  3. Store on a magnetic rack or in a sheath. Loose in a drawer is the fastest way to dull a fine edge.

The whetstones collection has the 1000/3000 combo I recommend, and the kitchen knife steel types guide covers which steels behave which way on the stone.

Is the Damascus pattern around the VG10 core just for show?

The pattern is mostly cosmetic, but the san mai construction underneath is structural and matters a great deal. "San mai" means a hard cutting core sandwiched between two softer cladding layers. The hard core holds the edge. The cladding absorbs shock and lets the maker run the core at higher hardness than would be safe in a single-piece blade. The 67 or 73-layer counts describe the cladding folds, not the core. The pattern itself does not improve cutting performance, but the san mai underneath is what allows VG10 to run at 62 HRC without the edge becoming fragile in normal use. Our piece on real Damascus versus fake Damascus covers how to tell genuine forge-welded san mai from acid-etched fakes.

What are the honest limitations of VG10?

It is not the peak of edge retention. If you sharpen knives weekly and can tell the difference between 60 HRC and 64 HRC under the same geometry, powder steels like 14Cr14MoVNb, SG2 or ZDP-189 will hold a fine edge longer between touch-ups. Their wear-resistance ceiling is higher because the carbide structure is finer and more uniform. For most home cooks the difference is invisible.

It is not the toughest steel in its hardness range either. VG10 has moderate toughness, comparable to AUS-10 and CPM-S35VN. If you want maximum chip resistance against accidental impact, a softer, less carbide-rich steel like 14C28N or AEB-L at lower hardness will out-tough it. The trade-off is they lose their factory edge faster.

And it is not magic. A poorly heat-treated VG10 blade will be out-performed by a properly heat-treated 1.4116 blade. Composition is the recipe, heat treatment is the cooking, and buying outside an established forge with verifiable protocols means gambling on the metallurgy regardless of what the steel is called.

Sources and further reading

  • Larrin Thomas, VG10 and Super Gold 2: Takefu Stainless Steel Properties and History, Knife Steel Nerds. History, composition and corrosion resistance data.
  • Larrin Thomas, Knife Steels Rated by a Metallurgist, Knife Steel Nerds. Toughness and edge-retention benchmarking against CPM-S45VN at 61 Rc.
  • Larrin Thomas, Testing the Edge Retention of 48 Knife Steels, Knife Steel Nerds. CATRA TCC framework for steel comparison.
  • zKnives steel reference, VG10 composition graph and equivalents.

Related articles

  • VG10 vs 10Cr15CoMoV: The Truth About Modern Kitchen Knife Steels
  • Kitchen Knife Steel Types Explained
  • Knife Steel Hardness Guide: What HRC Numbers Actually Mean
  • Real Damascus Steel vs Fake: How to Tell the Difference

Frequently Asked Questions

Is VG10 a Japanese knife steel?

Yes, VG10 is a Japanese steel made by Takefu Special Steel in Fukui Prefecture, first developed around 1959. The steel itself is Japanese; the knife built around it can be made anywhere. Xinzuo and Hezhen knives are forged in Yangjiang, China, using either Takefu VG10 directly or its near-identical Chinese counterpart 10Cr15CoMoV as the cutting core.

What hardness is VG10 at?

VG10 is most commonly run at 60 to 62 HRC on the Rockwell C scale, which is the practical sweet spot for kitchen cutting steel. Hard enough to hold a 12 to 15 degree edge for two to three months of daily use, soft enough to flex slightly under impact instead of chipping, easy enough to resharpen on a 1000-grit whetstone.

Is VG10 better than 10Cr15CoMoV?

The two steels share a near-identical chemical recipe of around 1% carbon, 15% chromium, 1.5% cobalt, 1% molybdenum and 0.2% vanadium, and they perform the same way at the cutting board when heat treated correctly. VG10 has the longer manufacturing track record. 10Cr15CoMoV is the cheaper alloy twin produced to the Chinese GB standard. In side-by-side daily use the differences are smaller than the differences between two samples of the same steel from different makers.

How long does a VG10 edge last?

For a serious home cook using the knife daily on vegetables, fish, herbs and boneless protein, expect the working edge to last 8 to 12 weeks before it needs more than a ceramic-rod hone. The factory-fresh edge that arrives in the box, where the blade glides through onion paper without flexing it, lasts roughly three to four weeks of daily use. After that you are still cutting cleanly, just with a small amount of forward pressure.

Does VG10 rust?

Not under normal kitchen use. With around 15% chromium and 1% molybdenum the steel forms a stable passive oxide layer that resists kitchen acids and salt. The most likely cause of spotting on a VG10 blade is leaving it wet on a damp board for hours or storing it dirty in a humid drawer. Hand wash, dry immediately, and the steel will stay bright for years.

Can I use a steel honing rod on a VG10 knife?

No. A traditional steel honing rod can microchip the edge of any cutting steel running above 58 HRC, and VG10 runs at 60 to 62 HRC. Use a ceramic honing rod instead. It realigns the edge gently without damaging the harder core, and it adds weeks between full whetstone sharpenings.

articles.title: Nakiri vs Santoku: Which Vegetable Knife Wins?
articles.body_html:
What Is the Real Difference Between a Nakiri and a Santoku?

Quick answer: A nakiri (菜切, "vegetable cutter") has a flat, rectangular blade with a square tip and is built for one cutting motion: straight up and down push-cuts. A santoku (三徳, "three virtues") has a sheep's-foot tip and a slightly curved belly, so it handles push-cuts and a relaxed rock-chop. Both run 165 to 180 mm long, both use Japanese-grade steel at 60 HRC and above, and both are lighter and sharper than a Western chef knife. If 80% of your prep is vegetables, the nakiri is faster. If you want one knife that also handles fish and boneless protein, the santoku is the better pick.

I get this question a lot from customers who already own a santoku and are wondering whether a nakiri is worth adding, or who are picking their first Japanese-style knife and stuck between the two. Most of the comparison articles online treat it as a coin flip. It is not. The two knives reward genuinely different cooking styles, and choosing by personality fit instead of "versatility" gets you a knife you actually enjoy using.

I run xinzuo.com.au, the Australian distributor for Xinzuo and Hezhen knives. I visited the Yangjiang factory earlier this year and have tested every nakiri and santoku in our catalogue across a few months of home cooking. What follows is the comparison the way I would walk a friend through it, with specs straight from the catalogue and the trade-offs no one else writes down.

What Does the Blade Profile Actually Change?

The blade profile is the whole game. A nakiri's edge is a straight line from heel to tip with a square end, so the entire cutting edge contacts the board at the same time on every stroke. A santoku has a slight upward curve toward the tip and a rounded sheep's-foot end, which keeps the heel on the board while the tip lifts a few millimetres at the end of each push.

Those two shapes drive completely different cutting techniques.

With a nakiri, you push-cut. Drop the blade straight down through the food with a small forward motion at the bottom of the stroke. The flat edge cleaves the entire piece in one go, no rocking. When you are slicing 30 spring onions or julienning a daikon, you can set up a rhythm that feels closer to a guillotine than a knife. The cuts are uniform because the geometry forces them to be.

With a santoku, you push-cut for most things and lazy-rock for the rest. The curve at the tip means you can keep the front of the blade on the board, lift the heel, and chop down through herbs or garlic without lifting the whole knife. It is a gentler version of the rocking motion you use with a Western chef knife, and it is the technique most home cooks default to without thinking.

The physics here are not just opinion. Atkins, Xu and Jeronimidis (2004) measured cutting forces and found that adding a small forward slicing motion to a downward press, at a slice-push ratio of around 3:1, reduced the required downward force to roughly one-tenth. The nakiri's flat profile naturally produces that motion on every stroke. With a santoku, you get it on push-cuts and lose it the moment you start rocking, because the rocking motion is mostly downward force. That is why a nakiri feels lighter through vegetables even though the two knives weigh almost the same.

How Do the Specs Compare Side by Side?

The nakiri and santoku are closer on paper than people expect, but the small differences matter in the hand.

Spec Nakiri Santoku
Blade length (typical) 165 to 180 mm (6.5 to 7") 165 to 190 mm (6.5 to 7.5")
Edge profile Dead flat, square tip Mostly flat, gentle curve to sheep's-foot tip
Edge angle (per side) 10 to 15 degrees 10 to 15 degrees
Spine thickness 1.8 to 2.2 mm 1.5 to 2.2 mm
Hardness (HRC) 60 to 64 (powder steels up to 67) 60 to 64 (powder steels up to 67)
Blade height (heel to spine) 50 to 60 mm 42 to 50 mm
Typical weight 160 to 200 g 150 to 190 g
Cutting motion Push-cut only Push-cut and gentle rock-chop
Best for Vegetables, herbs, batch prep Vegetables, fish, boneless protein, mixed prep
What it cannot do Pierce, score skin, fine tip work, bone, frozen food Bone, frozen food, heavy splitting

Two specs are doing most of the work here. The first is blade height. A nakiri is taller than a santoku, usually by 8 to 15 mm at the heel. That extra height does two things: it gives you knuckle clearance on a board so you can chop fast without scraping your fingers, and it turns the wide blade face into a bench scraper for moving prepped vegetables to the pan. The santoku is a bit shallower, which makes it more nimble for fine work but slightly less effective at the big sweep-and-scoop.

The second is the tip. The nakiri has no tip at all. The blade ends in a square corner. You cannot pierce a tomato skin, score a fillet, or pick out an avocado pit with it. The santoku has a sheep's-foot tip, which is rounded but still pointed enough for light tip work like pricking sausages or scoring chicken skin. If you only have one knife, the santoku does jobs the nakiri physically cannot.

Which Knife Is Faster for Vegetable Prep?

The nakiri, by a clear margin, on any task that involves repetitive cutting of the same shape. Push-cut testing across multiple knife reviewers and prep-cook timing trials puts the nakiri 10 to 20% faster than a santoku of the same length on jobs like julienning daikon, dicing onions, and shredding cabbage. The flat blade is the reason. Every stroke moves the entire edge through the food at the same time, so you finish a slice in one motion instead of pivoting to the tip.

Where the gap closes is on mixed prep. If you are dicing an onion, then breaking down a chicken thigh, then mincing parsley, the santoku's tip and gentle curve let you switch tasks without changing knives. The nakiri can mince herbs, but it cannot pivot, score skin, or tip-cut the way a santoku can. So a santoku finishes a varied prep job faster than a nakiri does, even though the nakiri is faster on each individual vegetable subtask.

This is the right way to think about it. Pick the nakiri if your weeknight cooking is dominated by long vegetable prep sessions: stir-fries, roast veg trays, ratatouille, kimchi, soups, big salads. Pick the santoku if you spread your prep across vegetables, fish, and meat in roughly equal parts.

What About Herbs and Mincing?

Both knives mince herbs well, but they want different techniques. With a nakiri, you push-cut through a tight pile of basil, then use the wide blade as a scraper to gather and re-stack the pile, then push-cut again. The cuts are clean, the leaves do not bruise, and the wide face moves them into a bowl in one swipe.

With a santoku, you can rock-mince through the pile by anchoring the curved tip on the board and pivoting the heel. It is faster on small piles. On larger piles you still need to push-cut and stack, the same as a nakiri. For day-to-day herb work most home cooks do, both are fine. For Italian or Lebanese cooking that involves big quantities of parsley and mint, the nakiri's flat-blade-as-scraper combo wins.

Why Does a Sharp Knife Matter More Than You Think?

This holds for both shapes, but it is worth saying because both knives live or die on edge sharpness. Wu et al. (2025) published a study in PNAS measuring aerosol release during onion cutting. A dull blade with a tip radius above 13 micrometres produced roughly 40 times more tear-inducing droplets than a sharp blade with a tip radius below 1 micrometre. The dull blade crushes cells instead of slicing them, launching irritant compounds at up to 40 metres per second.

The takeaway: sharpness is a food quality issue, not a performance one. A blunt nakiri or santoku bruises basil, browns lettuce, and weeps tomato juice everywhere. A sharp one preserves the cell walls, the colour, and the flavour. Both knives need a ceramic honing rod between sharpenings and a 1000 to 3000 grit whetstone every two to four months with regular use.

How Does Steel Choice Change the Pick?

Both knives use the same Japanese-grade steels, so the steel choice is about how much maintenance you want, not which shape you should buy. Three steels cover almost every nakiri and santoku in our catalogue.

Steel HRC Edge retention Best for
German 1.4116 56 to 58 Good Beginners, low-maintenance home cooks
10Cr15CoMoV 60 to 62 Very good Serious home cooks. Sweet spot.
14Cr14MoVNb (powder steel) 62 to 64 Excellent Cooks who sharpen on a whetstone

10Cr15CoMoV is the steel I point most customers at. It is a Chinese-made high-carbon stainless with cobalt for hot hardness and molybdenum for edge stability, and the composition is near-identical to Japanese VG-10. Same chromium, same molybdenum, slightly more cobalt. It hits the sharpness ceiling that matters for vegetable work and is forgiving enough that you do not need to be a sharpening hobbyist to keep it good. We did a full VG-10 vs 10Cr15CoMoV breakdown if you want the chemistry detail.

14Cr14MoVNb is the upgrade. It is a powder-metallurgy stainless, which means the steel is atomised into a fine powder before being sintered into a billet. The result is a finer grain structure that takes a sharper apex and resists wear better than conventionally cast steel. Edge retention runs roughly two to three times longer than 10Cr15CoMoV. The trade-off is it wants a whetstone for sharpening, not a pull-through.

German 1.4116 at 56 to 58 HRC sits at the entry point. It does not hold the same acute edge that the harder steels do, but it is forgiving if you accidentally hit a chicken bone, and you can hone it on any steel rod. Good first knife if maintenance is a deal-breaker.

Why Does San Mai Construction Matter on Both Knives?

San mai (三枚, "three layers") is what stops a hard, thin Japanese-style blade from snapping the first time you torque it sideways in a pumpkin. A hard cutting core forms the actual edge, and two softer stainless cladding layers sandwich it. The core holds the sharp edge for a long time. The soft cladding flexes under impact and absorbs lateral shock that would chip a monosteel hard blade.

Both nakiri and santoku in our catalogue use san mai construction with either 67-layer or 73-layer Damascus cladding. Each fold of the cladding doubles the layer count and adds a thin barrier of structural toughness around the core. The wave pattern on the blade face is functional, not just decorative, although it does also look good. A Lan Series 73-layer powder-steel blade has a different chip resistance profile to a Mo Series 67-layer 10Cr15CoMoV blade, and both are tougher than a monosteel knife at the same hardness would be.

Worth knowing: San mai construction is the reason you can buy a 64 HRC nakiri for under $150 and not worry about it shattering. The hard core stays sharp. The soft cladding takes the hits. Stay off bone and frozen food and the blade will last decades.

Which One Should You Buy?

The recommendation that most articles dodge: pick by cooking style, not by "which is more versatile." Both are versatile within the work they were built for, and forcing one into the other's job is what makes people unhappy with their knife.

Your cooking style Best fit
Mostly vegetables: stir-fries, salads, roast veg trays, soups Nakiri
Mixed prep: vegetables plus fish or boneless protein Santoku
You batch prep on weekends and want speed Nakiri
You rock-chop out of habit and do not want to relearn Santoku
First Japanese-style knife, want one that does most things Santoku
You already own a chef knife or gyuto and want a vegetable specialist Nakiri

The honest framing: a nakiri is the better knife for the job it was designed for, but a santoku is the better one-knife solution. If you are weighing your second Japanese-style knife and you already have a chef knife or a gyuto for protein work, get the nakiri. The push-cut speed is something you genuinely feel within five minutes of using it. If you want one knife that does 90% of home cooking and you are not ready to add a chef knife or a cleaver to the kit, get the santoku.

Which Xinzuo Knives Should I Actually Look At?

These are the picks I would put in front of a friend asking the same question. Specs come straight from the catalogue, prices are AUD, and every blade was forged in Yangjiang from Japanese-grade steel.

Best Entry-Level Pair

Best entry-level santoku: Supreme Series 7" Santoku (X02). German 1.4116 steel at 56 to 58 HRC, $39.95. The cheapest way into a Japanese-style shape. Hone it with a regular steel rod, sharpen on whatever you have, and it will not chip if you accidentally clip a chicken bone. It is not the sharpest knife in the catalogue, but at this price it is the easiest yes for someone who wants to try the shape before committing.

Best entry-level nakiri: Supreme Series 7" Nakiri (X02). Same German 1.4116 steel at 56 to 58 HRC, $49.95. Same low-maintenance story, same forgiving hardness. If you have never push-cut before and want to learn the technique on a $50 knife, this is the one.

Best Mid-Range Pair (10Cr15CoMoV)

Mid-range santoku: Mo Series 7.5" Santoku (X06). 10Cr15CoMoV core at 60 to 62 HRC, 67-layer Damascus, G10 handle, $94.95. The VG-10 equivalent at a Chinese-made price. Sharper out of the box, holds its edge two to three times longer than German steel, and the san mai construction makes it tough despite the harder core.

Mid-range nakiri: Mo Series 6.8" Nakiri (X06). Same 10Cr15CoMoV core at 60 to 62 HRC, 67-layer Damascus, $94.95. Matched pair if you want the santoku and nakiri to share steel and look. Push-cuts noticeably faster than the santoku once you settle into the technique.

Best Powder-Steel Pair

Powder-steel santoku: Lan Series 7" Santoku (B37). 14Cr14MoVNb powder steel at 62 to 64 HRC, 73-layer Damascus, olive wood handle, $129.95. Sharper apex and longer edge retention than 10Cr15CoMoV. Pair it with a basic 1000/3000 grit whetstone and you will sharpen it two or three times a year, ten minutes each time.

Powder-steel nakiri: Lan Series 7" Nakiri (B37). Same 14Cr14MoVNb core at 62 to 64 HRC, same 73-layer Damascus, same olive wood handle, $134.95. The vegetable specialist done properly. If 80% of your cooking is plant-forward and you sharpen your own knives, this is the one I would buy.

For the Cook Who Wants Both

If you want to skip the choice and own both, my pick is the Mo Series 6.8" Nakiri and the Mo Series 7.5" Santoku. Same steel, same Damascus pattern, same G10 handle, $189.90 for the pair. The nakiri handles the long batch sessions and the santoku covers the tip-work the nakiri cannot. They sit on the magnetic rack like a matched set and they cover almost every prep job between them.

For more on choosing the right knife for vegetable-heavy cooking, our vegetable knife buying guide walks through the wider options including cleavers and Western chef knives. If you want to go deeper on the nakiri specifically, the full nakiri knife guide covers history, technique, and which length suits which kitchen. And if you are still weighing a santoku against a Western chef knife instead, the santoku vs chef knife article compares those two head-to-head. The Japanese-style knife types explained article covers nakiri, santoku, gyuto, bunka, and the rest of the family if you want the full map.

How Do You Maintain a Nakiri or Santoku?

Both knives need the same care, and it is not a lot. Hone the edge on a ceramic rod every few uses (a steel rod can microchip the harder Japanese-grade edge). Sharpen on a whetstone, ideally 1000 grit for setting the edge and 3000 grit for finishing, every two to four months with regular use. Hand wash and dry immediately. Never put it in the dishwasher. Store on a magnetic rack, in a knife block, or with a saya (blade guard), not loose in a drawer.

Five things will damage either knife: bone, frozen food, lateral twisting, the dishwasher, and storing loose with other utensils. The hard steel that holds the acute edge is the same steel that chips if you whack it sideways. If you find yourself reaching for one of those tasks, grab a heavy German chef knife or a cleaver instead. That is what professional kitchens do, and it is what I do at home.

The Western chef knife earns its keep on bone-in cuts and dense produce. The nakiri and santoku earn theirs on the daily prep that fills 90% of home cooking. Use the right tool for the job and both knives will last decades.

Where Do Nakiri and Santoku Come From?

The nakiri is the older of the two. Its predecessor, the kamagata usuba, dates back to the 17th century in Edo-period Japan. The shape was refined over the next 300 years into the modern nakiri: a thin, flat, double-bevel vegetable cleaver designed for home cooks. The name nakiri (菜切) literally means "vegetable cutter," and that is what it has always been built for. It never had to do anything else, which is part of why the geometry is so optimised.

The santoku is much younger. It emerged in the Kansai region of Japan in the 1940s, after World War II, when Western food (particularly meat) became common in Japanese households. Traditional Japanese kitchens used separate knives for each ingredient: the nakiri for vegetables, the deba for fish, the yanagiba for slicing. The santoku combined elements of all three into a single all-purpose blade. The name (三徳) means "three virtues," though whether that refers to three ingredients (vegetables, fish, meat) or three cutting techniques (slicing, dicing, mincing) is still debated.

The Xinzuo and Hezhen versions are forged in Yangjiang, China, the city that has been Asia's blade-making hub for more than 1,400 years. The steel cores hit the same composition and heat-treatment specs as VG-10, SG2, and similar Japanese steels. They are not stamped "Made in Japan" because they were not made there, and we are upfront about that. The cutting performance matches Japanese-made knives at the same hardness and geometry. The price does not, because Yangjiang labour costs are lower and we sell direct as the local distributor. A Japanese-made nakiri or santoku at the same spec runs $400 to $700 in Australia. Our equivalents run $90 to $300.

Shop Nakiri Knives Shop Santoku Knives Shop All Japanese-style Knives

Sources

  • Atkins, A.G., Xu, X. and Jeronimidis, G. (2004). "Cutting, by 'pressing and slicing,' of thin floppy slices of materials." Journal of Materials Science, 39, 2761 to 2766.
  • Wu, Z., Hooshanginejad, A., Wang, W., Hui, C.-Y. and Jung, S. (2025). "Droplet outbursts from onion cutting." Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 122(42).
  • Larrin Thomas, Knife Steel Nerds. CATRA edge retention testing, edge angle vs cutting performance.

Related reading

  • Nakiri Knife Guide: The Japanese Vegetable Cleaver Explained
  • Japanese-style Knife Types Explained: Santoku, Nakiri, Gyuto and More
  • Best Knife for Vegetables: An Australian Buying Guide
  • Santoku vs Chef Knife: Which One Should You Buy?

Frequently Asked Questions

Is a nakiri better than a santoku for vegetables?

Yes, on dedicated vegetable prep. The nakiri's flat blade contacts the entire cutting board on every push-cut, which is 10 to 20% faster than a santoku on tasks like julienning, dicing, and shredding. The santoku's slight curve makes it more flexible across mixed prep, but on pure vegetable speed the nakiri wins. If most of your cooking is plant-forward, the nakiri is the right pick.

Can a santoku do everything a nakiri can?

Almost. A santoku will dice, mince, and slice vegetables well, but it will not match the nakiri's push-cut speed on long batch prep. Where the santoku has the edge is on tasks the nakiri physically cannot do: piercing tomato skin, scoring chicken, tip-work in tight spaces. If you want one knife to handle everything, the santoku is the better all-rounder. If you want the fastest vegetable knife, the nakiri wins.

What size nakiri or santoku should I buy?

165 to 180 mm (6.5 to 7 inch) for both, for almost every Australian home cook. Most kitchen benches and cutting boards accommodate this length, and the lighter total weight reduces fatigue during long prep. Go to 190 mm (7.5 inch) if you batch prep on weekends or have larger hands. Drop to 165 mm (6.5 inch) if you have small hands or do mostly fine prep. Anything shorter than 6.5 inch starts feeling like a utility knife.

Are nakiri and santoku knives Japanese?

The shapes are Japanese in origin. Xinzuo and Hezhen knives carrying these shapes are forged in Yangjiang, China, using Japanese-grade steels (10Cr15CoMoV, 14Cr14MoVNb) and Japanese forging techniques (san mai lamination, cryogenic quench). The cutting performance matches Japanese-made knives at the same hardness and geometry, but the knives are not Japanese-made. We frame them as Japanese-style, not Japanese.

Can you use a nakiri or santoku on bone or frozen food?

No. Both knives use harder steel (60 to 64 HRC) at acute edge angles (10 to 15 degrees per side), which makes them sharp but more chip-prone than a softer Western chef knife. Hitting bone or frozen food can chip the edge and require whetstone repair. Keep a cheap thick German-style chef knife or a cleaver for those tasks and use the nakiri or santoku for everything else.

What edge angle should I sharpen a nakiri or santoku at?

10 to 15 degrees per side, both knives. Use a 1000 grit whetstone to set the edge and 3000 grit to refine it. A ceramic honing rod (not a steel rod) keeps the edge aligned between sharpenings. Most cooks need to sharpen a nakiri or santoku in 10Cr15CoMoV or 14Cr14MoVNb steel two to four times a year with regular home use. Xinzuo nakiri and santoku knives ship with a 12 to 15 degree factory edge.
